finding.summary: Bass track is gain-staged hot: fader sits at +5 dB while the post-fader true peak is only -0.3 dBTP, leaving essentially no headroom for downstream bus processing. The 1/3-octave spectrum is sparse but shows 4 kHz at -24 dB sitting 6 dB above the 1 kHz band at -30 dB — an unusually high-frequency tilt for a bass with no FX chain — but three data points are too few to target an EQ move.
finding.probable_cause: Track volume fader is set 5 dB above unity, which pushes the captured signal within 0.3 dB of the inter-sample ceiling. The empty FX chain means nothing downstream is catching transients, and the healthy 12 dB crest factor confirms peaks (not sustained level) are what is hitting the ceiling.
finding.confidence_reason: The headroom calculation follows directly from true_peak_db and volume_db and is reliable (silence_fraction 0.05 — level stats are not diluted). The 4 kHz observation is real but cannot be acted on with only three 1/3-octave bands; I therefore mention it but do not propose an EQ change.

evidence_refs.description: -0.3 dBTP — only 0.3 dB below the inter-sample ceiling.
+5 dB fader above unity on an empty FX chain.
-0.7 dBFS — 0.4 dB below true peak, consistent with minimal inter-sample overshoot (no heavy limiting evident).
12 dB — healthy, transient peaks are what is hitting the ceiling, not sustained RMS.
4 kHz at -24 dB vs 1 kHz at -30 dB; spectral tilt noted but resolution is too coarse for a specific EQ proposal.
Empty — no downstream limiting or compression to manage peaks.

proposal.reason: Trim the bass fader by 2 dB to recover headroom for mix-bus summing and any downstream processing. Crest factor is 12 dB and integrated LUFS is -20, so a 2 dB cut preserves dynamic shape and body; the resulting true peak should land around -2.3 dBTP, giving ~2 dB of usable margin without changing the mix balance meaningfully.
